Ōmine Head Shrine ([[Shugendō]])
[[Gōsha]] (local shrine) in Shinto | consecration_year = Unknown | Main hall = | Alternate name = Ōmine Head Shrine, Yoshino-Kumano Shrine (intermediate between Yoshino and Kumano)
Tenkawa Benzaiten, Tsubouchi Benzaiten Shrine
[[Biwazan Byakuhakuji]] (formerly affiliated with [[Kōfuku-ji]]) | Festival = [[July 17]] }} '''Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine''' (天河大弁財天社), commonly referred to as '''Tenkawa Shrine''', is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the [[Yoshino District]], [[Nara Prefecture]]. The shrine is renowned for its deep association with the arts, music, and spirituality. It is a popular pilgrimage site for those in creative professions and seekers of spiritual insight. == Enshrined deity == The shrine's primary deity is [[Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto]], one of the [[Munakata Three Goddesses]]. Originally, the shrine was dedicated to [[Benzaiten]] (the Japanese Buddhist equivalent of the Hindu goddess [[Saraswati]]). Following the [[Shinto-Buddhism separation]] (神仏分離) during the Meiji period, the deity was renamed to align with Shinto practices while retaining Benzaiten's legacy. The shrine is often considered one of the "[[Three Great Shrines of Benzaiten]]" (along with [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[Chikubu Island|Chikubushima Shrine]]) and is sometimes included among the "Five Great Benzaiten Shrines of Japan." == History == The shrine traces its origins to the [[Asuka period]] and is closely associated with [[Shugendō]] practices and the legendary ascetic [[En no Gyōja]], who established [[Ōmine-san]] as a sacred site. [[Kūkai]] (the founder of [[Kōyasan]]) also revered the shrine and reportedly spent three years here in spiritual practice before establishing [[Mount Kōya]]. The shrine's present structure dates back to the reign of [[Emperor Tenmu]], who prayed for victory during the [[Jinshin War]]. Following his triumph, Emperor Tenmu contributed to the shrine’s expansion, solidifying its significance. Historically, the site was referred to as '''Biwazan Byakuhakuji''', a Buddhist temple dedicated to Benzaiten. During the [[Edo period]], it was highly regarded as a place of worship for the [[Southern Court of Japan]], further solidifying its status as a spiritual hub. == Cultural Significance == The shrine is known for its unique connection to traditional [[Noh]] theater and music. It houses numerous offerings related to the performing arts, and its annual [[July 16]]–[[July 17]] festival includes sacred performances and rituals. The sacred statue of Benzaiten enshrined in the main hall is normally kept hidden and is only revealed during the annual grand festival. Another sacred statue, known as the "Sun Wheel Benzaiten," is unveiled every 60 years but was last displayed in 2008 as part of a special commemoration. == Sacred Site and Facilities == The shrine's grounds include: - The **Five Shrines Hall**, symbolizing cosmic harmony. - A path leading to the summit of [[Mount Misen]] (1,895 meters), which serves as the shrine's [[Okunomiya]] (inner sanctuary). == Important Cultural Properties == - **Wooden Noh and Kyōgen Masks**: A collection of 30 masks designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government. == Festivals and Rituals == Key events include: - **New Year Ceremony (January 1)**: Blessings for the year ahead. - **Misen Inner Shrine Festival (May)**: Rituals performed at the summit of Mount Misen. - **Grand Festival (July 16–17)**: Sacred performances and prayers. - **Autumn Festival (November 2)**: Celebrates harvest and gratitude. == Access == - By bus: From [[Shimoichiguchi Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], take the Nara Kotsu Bus to "Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine" (a11rox. 1 hour). - By car: Drive south along [[National Route 309]] and follow signs to the shrine. == See Also == - [[Ōmine Mountains]] - [[Shugendō]] - [[Benzaiten]] - [[Yoshino-Kumano National Park]] == External Links == * [http://www.tenkawa-jinja.or.jp/ Official website] * [http://www.naraken-jinjacho.jp/jinja13.html Torii gate {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|frame-height=250|coord={{coord|34|13|24|N|135|50|30|E}}}} | location = [[Tsubouchi]], [[Tenkawa]], [[Yoshino District]], [[Nara Prefecture]], Japan | Latitude degrees = 34| Latitude minutes = 13| Latitude seconds = 24.0 | Longitude degrees = 135| Longitude minutes = 50| Longitude seconds = 30.0 | deity = [[Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto]] | Shrine rank = Yoshino General Shrine
Ōmine Head Shrine ([[Shugendō]])
[[Gōsha]] (local shrine) in Shinto | consecration_year = Unknown | Main hall = | Alternate name = Ōmine Head Shrine, Yoshino-Kumano Shrine (intermediate between Yoshino and Kumano)
Tenkawa Benzaiten, Tsubouchi Benzaiten Shrine
[[Biwazan Byakuhakuji]] (formerly affiliated with [[Kōfuku-ji]]) | Festival = [[July 17]] }} '''Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine''' (天河大弁財天社), commonly referred to as '''Tenkawa Shrine''', is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the [[Yoshino District]], [[Nara Prefecture]]. The shrine is renowned for its deep association with the arts, music, and spirituality. It is a popular pilgrimage site for those in creative professions and seekers of spiritual insight. == Enshrined deity == The shrine's primary deity is [[Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto]], one of the [[Munakata Three Goddesses]]. Originally, the shrine was dedicated to [[Benzaiten]] (the Japanese Buddhist equivalent of the Hindu goddess [[Saraswati]]). Following the [[Shinto-Buddhism separation]] (神仏分離) during the Meiji period, the deity was renamed to align with Shinto practices while retaining Benzaiten's legacy. The shrine is often considered one of the "[[Three Great Shrines of Benzaiten]]" (along with [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[Chikubu Island|Chikubushima Shrine]]) and is sometimes included among the "Five Great Benzaiten Shrines of Japan." == History == The shrine traces its origins to the [[Asuka period]] and is closely associated with [[Shugendō]] practices and the legendary ascetic [[En no Gyōja]], who established [[Ōmine-san]] as a sacred site. [[Kūkai]] (the founder of [[Kōyasan]]) also revered the shrine and reportedly spent three years here in spiritual practice before establishing [[Mount Kōya]]. The shrine's present structure dates back to the reign of [[Emperor Tenmu]], who prayed for victory during the [[Jinshin War]]. Following his triumph, Emperor Tenmu contributed to the shrine’s expansion, solidifying its significance. Historically, the site was referred to as '''Biwazan Byakuhakuji''', a Buddhist temple dedicated to Benzaiten. During the [[Edo period]], it was highly regarded as a place of worship for the [[Southern Court of Japan]], further solidifying its status as a spiritual hub. == Cultural Significance == The shrine is known for its unique connection to traditional [[Noh]] theater and music. It houses numerous offerings related to the performing arts, and its annual [[July 16]]–[[July 17]] festival includes sacred performances and rituals. The sacred statue of Benzaiten enshrined in the main hall is normally kept hidden and is only revealed during the annual grand festival. Another sacred statue, known as the "Sun Wheel Benzaiten," is unveiled every 60 years but was last displayed in 2008 as part of a special commemoration. == Sacred Site and Facilities == The shrine's grounds include: - The **Five Shrines Hall**, symbolizing cosmic harmony. - A path leading to the summit of [[Mount Misen]] (1,895 meters), which serves as the shrine's [[Okunomiya]] (inner sanctuary). == Important Cultural Properties == - **Wooden Noh and Kyōgen Masks**: A collection of 30 masks designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government. == Festivals and Rituals == Key events include: - **New Year Ceremony (January 1)**: Blessings for the year ahead. - **Misen Inner Shrine Festival (May)**: Rituals performed at the summit of Mount Misen. - **Grand Festival (July 16–17)**: Sacred performances and prayers. - **Autumn Festival (November 2)**: Celebrates harvest and gratitude. == Access == - By bus: From [[Shimoichiguchi Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], take the Nara Kotsu Bus to "Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine" (a11rox. 1 hour). - By car: Drive south along [[National Route 309]] and follow signs to the shrine.== See Also == - [[Ōmine Mountains]] - [[Shugendō]] - [[Benzaiten]] - [[Yoshino-Kumano National Park]] == External Links == * [http://www.tenkawa-jinja.or.jp/ Official website] * [http://www.naraken-jinjacho.jp/jinja13.html {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Tenkawa-dai-Benzaiten-sha 01.jpg|250px]]
Torii gate {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|frame-height=250|coord={{coord|34|13|24|N|135|50|30|E}}}} | location = [[Tsubouchi]], [[Tenkawa]], [[Yoshino District]], [[Nara Prefecture]], Japan | Latitude degrees = 34| Latitude minutes = 13| Latitude seconds = 24.0 | Longitude degrees = 135| Longitude minutes = 50| Longitude seconds = 30.0 | deity = [[Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto]] | Shrine rank = Yoshino General Shrine
Ōmine Head Shrine ([[Shugendō]])
[[Gōsha]] (local shrine) in Shinto | consecration_year = Unknown | Main hall = | Alternate name = Ōmine Head Shrine, Yoshino-Kumano Shrine (intermediate between Yoshino and Kumano)
Tenkawa Benzaiten, Tsubouchi Benzaiten Shrine
[[Biwazan Byakuhakuji]] (formerly affiliated with [[Kōfuku-ji]]) | Festival = [[July 17]] }} '''Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine''' (天河大弁財天社), commonly referred to as '''Tenkawa Shrine''', is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the [[Yoshino District]], [[Nara Prefecture]]. The shrine is renowned for its deep association with the arts, music, and spirituality. It is a popular pilgrimage site for those in creative professions and seekers of spiritual insight. == Enshrined deity == The shrine's primary deity is [[Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto]], one of the [[Munakata Three Goddesses]]. Originally, the shrine was dedicated to [[Benzaiten]] (the Japanese Buddhist equivalent of the Hindu goddess [[Saraswati]]). Following the [[Shinto-Buddhism separation]] (神仏分離) during the Meiji period, the deity was renamed to align with Shinto practices while retaining Benzaiten's legacy. The shrine is often considered one of the "[[Three Great Shrines of Benzaiten]]" (along with [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[Chikubu Island|Chikubushima Shrine]]) and is sometimes included among the "Five Great Benzaiten Shrines of Japan." == History == The shrine traces its origins to the [[Asuka period]] and is closely associated with [[Shugendō]] practices and the legendary ascetic [[En no Gyōja]], who established [[Ōmine-san]] as a sacred site. [[Kūkai]] (the founder of [[Kōyasan]]) also revered the shrine and reportedly spent three years here in spiritual practice before establishing [[Mount Kōya]]. The shrine's present structure dates back to the reign of [[Emperor Tenmu]], who prayed for victory during the [[Jinshin War]]. Following his triumph, Emperor Tenmu contributed to the shrine’s expansion, solidifying its significance. Historically, the site was referred to as '''Biwazan Byakuhakuji''', a Buddhist temple dedicated to Benzaiten. During the [[Edo period]], it was highly regarded as a place of worship for the [[Southern Court of Japan]], further solidifying its status as a spiritual hub. == Cultural Significance == The shrine is known for its unique connection to traditional [[Noh]] theater and music. It houses numerous offerings related to the performing arts, and its annual [[July 16]]–[[July 17]] festival includes sacred performances and rituals. The sacred statue of Benzaiten enshrined in the main hall is normally kept hidden and is only revealed during the annual grand festival. Another sacred statue, known as the "Sun Wheel Benzaiten," is unveiled every 60 years but was last displayed in 2008 as part of a special commemoration. == Sacred Site and Facilities == The shrine's grounds include: - The **Five Shrines Hall**, symbolizing cosmic harmony. - A path leading to the summit of [[Mount Misen]] (1,895 meters), which serves as the shrine's [[Okunomiya]] (inner sanctuary). == Important Cultural Properties == - **Wooden Noh and Kyōgen Masks**: A collection of 30 masks designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government. == Festivals and Rituals == Key events include: - **New Year Ceremony (January 1)**: Blessings for the year ahead. - **Misen Inner Shrine Festival (May)**: Rituals performed at the summit of Mount Misen. - **Grand Festival (July 16–17)**: Sacred performances and prayers. - **Autumn Festival (November 2)**: Celebrates harvest and gratitude. == Access == - By bus: From [[Shimoichiguchi Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], take the Nara Kotsu Bus to "Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine" (a11rox. 1 hour). - By car: Drive south along [[National Route 309]] and follow signs to the shrine. == See Also == - [[Ōmine Mountains]] - [[Shugendō]] - [[Benzaiten]] - [[Yoshino-Kumano National Park]] == External Links == * [http://www.tenkawa-jinja.or.jp/ Official website] * [http://www.naraken-jinjacho.jp/jinja13.html {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Tenkawa-dai-Benzaiten-sha 01.jpg|250px]]
Torii gate {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|frame-height=250|coord={{coord|34|13|24|N|135|50|30|E}}}} | location = [[Tsubouchi]], [[Tenkawa]], [[Yoshino District]], [[Nara Prefecture]], Japan | Latitude degrees = 34| Latitude minutes = 13| Latitude seconds = 24.0 | Longitude degrees = 135| Longitude minutes = 50| Longitude seconds = 30.0 | deity = [[Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto]] | Shrine rank = Yoshino General Shrine
Ōmine Head Shrine ([[Shugendō]])
[[Gōsha]] (local shrine) in Shinto | consecration_year = Unknown | Main hall = | Alternate name = Ōmine Head Shrine, Yoshino-Kumano Shrine (intermediate between Yoshino and Kumano)
Tenkawa Benzaiten, Tsubouchi Benzaiten Shrine
[[Biwazan Byakuhakuji]] (formerly affiliated with [[Kōfuku-ji]]) | Festival = [[July 17]] }} '''Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine''' (天河大弁財天社), commonly referred to as '''Tenkawa Shrine''', is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the [[Yoshino District]], [[Nara Prefecture]]. The shrine is renowned for its deep association with the arts, music, and spirituality. It is a popular pilgrimage site for those in creative professions and seekers of spiritual insight. == Enshrined deity == The shrine's primary deity is [[Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto]], one of the [[Munakata Three Goddesses]]. Originally, the shrine was dedicated to [[Benzaiten]] (the Japanese Buddhist equivalent of the Hindu goddess [[Saraswati]]). Following the [[Shinto-Buddhism separation]] (神仏分離) during the Meiji period, the deity was renamed to align with Shinto practices while retaining Benzaiten's legacy. The shrine is often considered one of the "[[Three Great Shrines of Benzaiten]]" (along with [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[Chikubu Island|Chikubushima Shrine]]) and is sometimes included among the "Five Great Benzaiten Shrines of Japan." == History == The shrine traces its origins to the [[Asuka period]] and is closely associated with [[Shugendō]] practices and the legendary ascetic [[En no Gyōja]], who established [[Ōmine-san]] as a sacred site. [[Kūkai]] (the founder of [[Kōyasan]]) also revered the shrine and reportedly spent three years here in spiritual practice before establishing [[Mount Kōya]]. The shrine's present structure dates back to the reign of [[Emperor Tenmu]], who prayed for victory during the [[Jinshin War]]. Following his triumph, Emperor Tenmu contributed to the shrine’s expansion, solidifying its significance. Historically, the site was referred to as '''Biwazan Byakuhakuji''', a Buddhist temple dedicated to Benzaiten. During the [[Edo period]], it was highly regarded as a place of worship for the [[Southern Court of Japan]], further solidifying its status as a spiritual hub. == Cultural Significance == The shrine is known for its unique connection to traditional [[Noh]] theater and music. It houses numerous offerings related to the performing arts, and its annual [[July 16]]–[[July 17]] festival includes sacred performances and rituals. The sacred statue of Benzaiten enshrined in the main hall is normally kept hidden and is only revealed during the annual grand festival. Another sacred statue, known as the "Sun Wheel Benzaiten," is unveiled every 60 years but was last displayed in 2008 as part of a special commemoration. == Sacred Site and Facilities == The shrine's grounds include: - The **Five Shrines Hall**, symbolizing cosmic harmony. - A path leading to the summit of [[Mount Misen]] (1,895 meters), which serves as the shrine's [[Okunomiya]] (inner sanctuary). == Important Cultural Properties == - **Wooden Noh and Kyōgen Masks**: A collection of 30 masks designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government. == Festivals and Rituals == Key events include: - **New Year Ceremony (January 1)**: Blessings for the year ahead. - **Misen Inner Shrine Festival (May)**: Rituals performed at the summit of Mount Misen. - **Grand Festival (July 16–17)**: Sacred performances and prayers. - **Autumn Festival (November 2)**: Celebrates harvest and gratitude. == Access == - By bus: From [[Shimoichiguchi Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], take the Nara Kotsu Bus to "Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine" (a11rox. 1 hour). - By car: Drive south along [[National Route 309]] and follow signs to the shrine. == See Also == - [[Ōmine Mountains]] - [[Shugendō]] - [[Benzaiten]] - [[Yoshino-Kumano National Park]] == External Links == * [http://www.tenkawa-jinja.or.jp/ Official website] * [http://www.naraken-jinjacho.jp/jinja13.html Nara Prefecture Shrine Directory] {{Munakata Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Nara Prefecture Shrines]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Shugendō]] [[Category:Benzaiten]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Yoshino District]] [[Category:Religious Tourism]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:return the see also]] }} [[sn:Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine]] [[ja:天河大弁財天社]] [[en:Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine}}

[[Kūkai]] (the founder of [[Kōyasan]]) also revered the shrine and reportedly spent three years here in spiritual practice before establishing [[Mount Kōya]]. The shrine's present structure dates back to the reign of [[Emperor Tenmu]], who prayed for victory during the [[Jinshin War]]. Following his triumph, Emperor Tenmu contributed to the shrine’s expansion, solidifying its significance. Historically, the site was referred to as '''Biwazan Byakuhakuji''', a Buddhist temple dedicated to Benzaiten. During the [[Edo period]], it was highly regarded as a place of worship for the [[Southern Court of Japan]], further solidifying its status as a spiritual hub. == Cultural Significance == The shrine is known for its unique connection to traditional [[Noh]] theater and music. It houses numerous offerings related to the performing arts, and its annual [[July 16]]–[[July 17]] festival includes sacred performances and rituals. The sacred statue of Benzaiten enshrined in the main hall is normally kept hidden and is only revealed during the annual grand festival. Another sacred statue, known as the "Sun Wheel Benzaiten," is unveiled every 60 years but was last displayed in 2008 as part of a special commemoration. == Sacred Site and Facilities == The shrine's grounds include: - The **Five Shrines Hall**, symbolizing cosmic harmony. - A path leading to the summit of [[Mount Misen]] (1,895 meters), which serves as the shrine's [[Okunomiya]] (inner sanctuary). == Important Cultural Properties == - **Wooden Noh and Kyōgen Masks**: A collection of 30 masks designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government. == Festivals and Rituals == Key events include: - **New Year Ceremony (January 1)**: Blessings for the year ahead. - **Misen Inner Shrine Festival (May)**: Rituals performed at the summit of Mount Misen. - **Grand Festival (July 16–17)**: Sacred performances and prayers. - **Autumn Festival (November 2)**: Celebrates harvest and gratitude. == Access == - By bus: From [[Shimoichiguchi Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], take the Nara Kotsu Bus to "Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine" (a11rox. 1 hour). - By car: Drive south along [[National Route 309]] and follow signs to the shrine. == See Also == - [[Ōmine Mountains]] - [[Shugendō]] - [[Benzaiten]] - [[Yoshino-Kumano National Park]] == External Links == * [http://www.tenkawa-jinja.or.jp/ Official website] * [http://www.naraken-jinjacho.jp/jinja13.html Nara Prefecture Shrine Directory] {{Munakata Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Nara Prefecture Shrines]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Shugendō]] [[Category:Benzaiten]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Yoshino District]] [[Category:Religious Tourism]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine]] [[ja:天河大弁財天社]] [[en:Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine}}

[[Biwazan Byakuhakuji]] (formerly affiliated with [[Kōfuku-ji]]) | Festival = [[July 17]] }} '''Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine''' (天河大弁財天社), commonly referred to as '''Tenkawa Shrine''', is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the [[Yoshino District]], [[Nara Prefecture]]. The shrine is renowned for its deep association with the arts, music, and spirituality. It is a popular pilgrimage site for those in creative professions and seekers of spiritual insight. == Enshrined deity == The shrine's primary deity is [[Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto]], one of the [[Munakata Three Goddesses]]. Originally, the shrine was dedicated to [[Benzaiten]] (the Japanese Buddhist equivalent of the Hindu goddess [[Saraswati]]). Following the [[Shinto-Buddhism separation]] (神仏分離) during the Meiji period, the deity was renamed to align with Shinto practices while retaining Benzaiten's legacy. The shrine is often considered one of the "[[Three Great Shrines of Benzaiten]]" (along with [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[Chikubu Island|Chikubushima Shrine]]) and is sometimes included among the "Five Great Benzaiten Shrines of Japan." == History == The shrine traces its origins to the [[Asuka period]] and is closely associated with [[Shugendō]] practices and the legendary ascetic [[En no Gyōja]], who established [[Ōmine-san]] as a sacred site. [[Kūkai]] (the founder of [[Kōyasan]]) also revered the shrine and reportedly spent three years here in spiritual practice before establishing [[Mount Kōya]]. The shrine's present structure dates back to the reign of [[Emperor Tenmu]], who prayed for victory during the [[Jinshin War]]. Following his triumph, Emperor Tenmu contributed to the shrine’s expansion, solidifying its significance. Historically, the site was referred to as '''Biwazan Byakuhakuji''', a Buddhist temple dedicated to Benzaiten. During the [[Edo period]], it was highly regarded as a place of worship for the [[Southern Court of Japan]], further solidifying its status as a spiritual hub. == Cultural Significance == The shrine is known for its unique connection to traditional [[Noh]] theater and music. It houses numerous offerings related to the performing arts, and its annual [[July 16]]–[[July 17]] festival includes sacred performances and rituals. The sacred statue of Benzaiten enshrined in the main hall is normally kept hidden and is only revealed during the annual grand festival. Another sacred statue, known as the "Sun Wheel Benzaiten," is unveiled every 60 years but was last displayed in 2008 as part of a special commemoration. == Sacred Site and Facilities == The shrine's grounds include: - The **Five Shrines Hall**, symbolizing cosmic harmony. - A path leading to the summit of [[Mount Misen]] (1,895 meters), which serves as the shrine's [[Okunomiya]] (inner sanctuary). == Important Cultural Properties == - **Wooden Noh and Kyōgen Masks**: A collection of 30 masks designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government. == Festivals and Rituals == Key events include: - **New Year Ceremony (January 1)**: Blessings for the year ahead. - **Misen Inner Shrine Festival (May)**: Rituals performed at the summit of Mount Misen. - **Grand Festival (July 16–17)**: Sacred performances and prayers. - **Autumn Festival (November 2)**: Celebrates harvest and gratitude. == Access == - By bus: From [[Shimoichiguchi Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], take the Nara Kotsu Bus to "Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine" (a11rox. 1 hour). - By car: Drive south along [[National Route 309]] and follow signs to the shrine. == See Also == - [[Ōmine Mountains]] - [[Shugendō]] - [[Benzaiten]] - [[Yoshino-Kumano National Park]] == External Links == * [http://www.tenkawa-jinja.or.jp/ Official website] * [http://www.naraken-jinjacho.jp/jinja13.html Nara Prefecture Shrine Directory] {{Munakata Faith}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Nara Prefecture Shrines]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Shugendō]] [[Category:Benzaiten]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Yoshino District]] [[Category:Religious Tourism]] [[Category:Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[sn:Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine]] [[ja:天河大弁財天社]] [[en:Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine}} {{translated page|ja|天河大弁財天社|version=100692558|comment=Imported full ja history}}

The shrine is renowned for its deep association with the arts, music, and spirituality. It is a popular pilgrimage site for those in creative professions and seekers of spiritual insight. == Enshrined deity == The shrine's primary deity is [[Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto]], one of the [[Munakata Three Goddesses]]. Originally, the shrine was dedicated to [[Benzaiten]] (the Japanese Buddhist equivalent of the Hindu goddess [[Saraswati]]). Following the [[Shinto-Buddhism separation]] (神仏分離) during the Meiji period, the deity was renamed to align with Shinto practices while retaining Benzaiten's legacy. Ōmine Head Shrine ([[Shugendō]])
[[Gōsha]] (local shrine) in Shinto | consecration_year = Unknown | Main hall = | Alternate name = Ōmine Head Shrine, Yoshino-Kumano Shrine (intermediate between Yoshino and Kumano)
Tenkawa Benzaiten, Tsubouchi Benzaiten Shrine
[[Biwazan Byakuhakuji]] (formerly affiliated with [[Kōfuku-ji]]) | Festival = [[July 17]] }} '''Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine''' (天河大弁財天社), commonly referred to as '''Tenkawa Shrine''', is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the [[Yoshino District]], [[Nara Prefecture]]. The shrine is renowned for its deep association with the arts, music, and spirituality. It is a popular pilgrimage site for those in creative professions and seekers of spiritual insight. == Enshrined deity == The shrine's primary deity is [[Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto]], one of the [[Three Munakata Goddesses|Munakata Three Goddesses]]. Originally, the shrine was dedicated to [[Benzaiten]] (the Japanese Buddhist equivalent of the Hindu goddess [[Saraswati]]). Following the [[Shinto-Buddhism separation]] (神仏分離) during the Meiji period, the deity was renamed to align with Shinto practices while retaining Benzaiten's legacy. The shrine is often considered one of the "[[Three Great Shrines of Benzaiten]]" (along with [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[Chikubu Island|Chikubushima Shrine]]) and is sometimes included among the "Five Great Benzaiten Shrines of Japan." == History == The shrine traces its origins to the [[Asuka period]] and is closely associated with [[Shugendō]] practices and the legendary ascetic [[En no Gyōja]], who established [[Ōmine-san]] as a sacred site. [[Kūkai]] (the founder of [[Kōyasan]]) also revered the shrine and reportedly spent three years here in spiritual practice before establishing [[Mount Kōya]]. The shrine's present structure dates back to the reign of [[Emperor Tenmu]], who prayed for victory during the [[Jinshin War]]. Following his triumph, Emperor Tenmu contributed to the shrine’s expansion, solidifying its significance. Historically, the site was referred to as '''Biwazan Byakuhakuji''', a Buddhist temple dedicated to Benzaiten. During the [[Edo period]], it was highly regarded as a place of worship for the [[Southern Court of Japan]], further solidifying its status as a spiritual hub. == Cultural Significance == The shrine is known for its unique connection to traditional [[Noh]] theater and music. It houses numerous offerings related to the performing arts, and its annual [[July 16]]–[[July 17]] festival includes sacred performances and rituals. The sacred statue of Benzaiten enshrined in the main hall is normally kept hidden and is only revealed during the annual grand festival. Another sacred statue, known as the "Sun Wheel Benzaiten," is unveiled every 60 years but was last displayed in 2008 as part of a special commemoration. == Sacred Site and Facilities == The shrine's grounds include: - The **Five Shrines Hall**, symbolizing cosmic harmony. - A path leading to the summit of [[Mount Misen]] (1,895 meters), which serves as the shrine's [[Okumiya|Okunomiya]] (inner sanctuary). == Important Cultural Properties == - **Wooden Noh and Kyōgen Masks**: A collection of 30 masks designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government. == Festivals and Rituals == Key events include: - **New Year Ceremony (January 1)**: Blessings for the year ahead. - **Misen Inner Shrine Festival (May)**: Rituals performed at the summit of Mount Misen. - **Grand Festival (July 16–17)**: Sacred performances and prayers. - **Autumn Festival (November 2)**: Celebrates harvest and gratitude. == Access == - By bus: From [[Shimoichiguchi Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], take the Nara Kotsu Bus to "Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine" (a11rox. 1 hour). - By car: Drive south along [[National Route 309]] and follow signs to the shrine. == See Also == - [[Ōmine Mountains]] - [[Shugendō]] - [[Benzaiten]] - [[Yoshino-Kumano National Park]] == External Links == * [http://www.tenkawa-jinja.or.jp/ Official website] * [http://www.naraken-jinjacho.jp/jinja13.html Nara Prefecture Shrine Directory] {{Munakata Faith}} {{translated page|ja|天河大弁財天社|version=100692558|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Nara Prefecture Shrines]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Shugendō]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[sn:Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine]] [[ja:天河大弁財天社]] [[en:Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine]] [[Category:天川村の建築物]]

[[Biwazan Byakuhakuji]] (formerly affiliated with [[Kōfuku-ji]]) | Festival = [[July 17]] }} '''Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine''' (天河大弁財天社), commonly referred to as '''Tenkawa Shrine''', is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the [[Yoshino District]], [[Nara Prefecture]]. The shrine is renowned for its deep association with the arts, music, and spirituality. It is a popular pilgrimage site for those in creative professions and seekers of spiritual insight. == Enshrined deity == The shrine's primary deity is [[Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto]], one of the [[Three Munakata Goddesses|Munakata Three Goddesses]]. Originally, the shrine was dedicated to [[Benzaiten]] (the Japanese Buddhist equivalent of the Hindu goddess [[Saraswati]]). Following the [[Shinto-Buddhism separation]] (神仏分離) during the Meiji period, the deity was renamed to align with Shinto practices while retaining Benzaiten's legacy. The shrine is often considered one of the "[[Three Great Shrines of Benzaiten]]" (along with [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[Chikubu Island|Chikubushima Shrine]]) and is sometimes included among the "Five Great Benzaiten Shrines of Japan." == History == The shrine traces its origins to the [[Asuka period]] and is closely associated with [[Shugendō]] practices and the legendary ascetic [[En no Gyōja]], who established [[Ōmine-san]] as a sacred site. [[Kūkai]] (the founder of [[Kōyasan]]) also revered the shrine and reportedly spent three years here in spiritual practice before establishing [[Mount Kōya]]. The shrine's present structure dates back to the reign of [[Emperor Tenmu]], who prayed for victory during the [[Jinshin War]]. Following his triumph, Emperor Tenmu contributed to the shrine’s expansion, solidifying its significance. Historically, the site was referred to as '''Biwazan Byakuhakuji''', a Buddhist temple dedicated to Benzaiten. During the [[Edo period]], it was highly regarded as a place of worship for the [[Southern Court of Japan]], further solidifying its status as a spiritual hub. == Cultural Significance == The shrine is known for its unique connection to traditional [[Noh]] theater and music. It houses numerous offerings related to the performing arts, and its annual [[July 16]]–[[July 17]] festival includes sacred performances and rituals. The sacred statue of Benzaiten enshrined in the main hall is normally kept hidden and is only revealed during the annual grand festival. Another sacred statue, known as the "Sun Wheel Benzaiten," is unveiled every 60 years but was last displayed in 2008 as part of a special commemoration. == Sacred Site and Facilities == The shrine's grounds include: - The **Five Shrines Hall**, symbolizing cosmic harmony. - A path leading to the summit of [[Mount Misen]] (1,895 meters), which serves as the shrine's [[Okumiya|Okunomiya]] (inner sanctuary). == Important Cultural Properties == - **Wooden Noh and Kyōgen Masks**: A collection of 30 masks designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government. == Festivals and Rituals == Key events include: - **New Year Ceremony (January 1)**: Blessings for the year ahead. - **Misen Inner Shrine Festival (May)**: Rituals performed at the summit of Mount Misen. - **Grand Festival (July 16–17)**: Sacred performances and prayers. - **Autumn Festival (November 2)**: Celebrates harvest and gratitude. == Access == - By bus: From [[Shimoichiguchi Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], take the Nara Kotsu Bus to "Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine" (a11rox. 1 hour). - By car: Drive south along [[National Route 309]] and follow signs to the shrine. == See Also == - [[Ōmine Mountains]] - [[Shugendō]] - [[Benzaiten]] - [[Yoshino-Kumano National Park]] == External Links == * [http://www.tenkawa-jinja.or.jp/ Official website] * [http://www.naraken-jinjacho.jp/jinja13.html Nara Prefecture Shrine Directory] {{Munakata Faith}} {{translated page|ja|天河大弁財天社|version=100692558|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Nara Prefecture Shrines]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Shugendō]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[sn:Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine]] [[ja:天河大弁財天社]] [[en:Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine]] [[Category:天川村の建築物]] [[Category:天川村の歴史]]

The shrine is often considered one of the "[[Three Great Shrines of Benzaiten]]" (along with [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[Chikubu Island|Chikubushima Shrine]]) and is sometimes included among the "Five Great Benzaiten Shrines of Japan." == History == The shrine traces its origins to the [[Asuka period]] and is closely associated with [[Shugendō]] practices and the legendary ascetic [[En no Gyōja]], who established [[Ōmine-san]] as a sacred site. [[Kūkai]] (the founder of [[Kōyasan]]) also revered the shrine and reportedly spent three years here in spiritual practice before establishing [[Mount Kōya]]. The shrine's present structure dates back to the reign of [[Emperor Tenmu]], who prayed for victory during the [[Jinshin War]]. Following his triumph, Emperor Tenmu contributed to the shrine’s expansion, solidifying its significance. Historically, the site was referred to as '''Biwazan Byakuhakuji''', a Buddhist temple dedicated to Benzaiten. During the [[Edo period]], it was highly regarded as a place of worship for the [[Southern Court of Japan]], further solidifying its status as a spiritual hub. == Cultural Significance == The shrine is known for its unique connection to traditional [[Noh]] theater and music. It houses numerous offerings related to the performing arts, and its annual [[July 16]]–[[July 17]] festival includes sacred performances and rituals. The sacred statue of Benzaiten enshrined in the main hall is normally kept hidden and is only revealed during the annual grand festival. Another sacred statue, known as the "Sun Wheel Benzaiten," is unveiled every 60 years but was last displayed in 2008 as part of a special commemoration. == Sacred Site and Facilities == The shrine's grounds include: - The **Five Shrines Hall**, symbolizing cosmic harmony. - A path leading to the summit of [[Mount Misen]] (1,895 meters), which serves as the shrine's [[Okumiya|Okunomiya]] (inner sanctuary). == Important Cultural Properties == - **Wooden Noh and Kyōgen Masks**: A collection of 30 masks designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government. == Festivals and Rituals == Key events include: - **New Year Ceremony (January 1)**: Blessings for the year ahead. - **Misen Inner Shrine Festival (May)**: Rituals performed at the summit of Mount Misen. - **Grand Festival (July 16–17)**: Sacred performances and prayers. - **Autumn Festival (November 2)**: Celebrates harvest and gratitude. == Access == - By bus: From [[Shimoichiguchi Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], take the Nara Kotsu Bus to "Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine" (a11rox. 1 hour). - By car: Drive south along [[National Route 309]] and follow signs to the shrine. == See Also == - The shrine is often considered one of the "[[Three Great Shrines of Benzaiten]]" (along with [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[Chikubu Island|Chikubushima Shrine]]) and is sometimes included among the "Five Great Benzaiten Shrines of Japan." == History == The shrine traces its origins to the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] and is closely associated with [[Shugendō]] practices and the legendary ascetic [[En no Gyōja]], who established [[Ōmine-san]] as a sacred site. [[Kūkai]] (the founder of [[Kōyasan]]) also revered the shrine and reportedly spent three years here in spiritual practice before establishing [[Mount Kōya]]. The shrine's present structure dates back to the reign of [[Emperor Tenmu]], who prayed for victory during the [[Jinshin War]]. Following his triumph, Emperor Tenmu contributed to the shrine’s expansion, solidifying its significance. Historically, the site was referred to as '''Biwazan Byakuhakuji''', a Buddhist temple dedicated to Benzaiten. During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], it was highly regarded as a place of worship for the [[Southern Court of Japan]], further solidifying its status as a spiritual hub. == Cultural Significance == The shrine is known for its unique connection to traditional [[Noh]] theater and music. It houses numerous offerings related to the performing arts, and its annual [[July 16]]–[[July 17]] festival includes sacred performances and rituals. The sacred statue of Benzaiten enshrined in the main hall is normally kept hidden and is only revealed during the annual grand festival. Another sacred statue, known as the "Sun Wheel Benzaiten," is unveiled every 60 years but was last displayed in 2008 as part of a special commemoration. == Sacred Site and Facilities == The shrine's grounds include: - The **Five Shrines Hall**, symbolizing cosmic harmony. - A path leading to the summit of [[Mount Misen]] (1,895 meters), which serves as the shrine's [[Okumiya|Okunomiya]] (inner sanctuary). == Important Cultural Properties == - **Wooden Noh and Kyōgen Masks**: A collection of 30 masks designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government. == Festivals and Rituals == Key events include: - **New Year Ceremony (January 1)**: Blessings for the year ahead. - **Misen Inner Shrine Festival (May)**: Rituals performed at the summit of Mount Misen. - **Grand Festival (July 16–17)**: Sacred performances and prayers. - **Autumn Festival (November 2)**: Celebrates harvest and gratitude. == Access == - By bus: From [[Shimoichiguchi Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], take the Nara Kotsu Bus to "Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine" (a11rox. 1 hour). - By car: Drive south along [[National Route 309]] and follow signs to the shrine. == See Also == - Originally, the shrine was dedicated to [[Benzaiten]] (the Japanese Buddhist equivalent of the Hindu goddess [[Saraswati]]). Following the [[Shinto-Buddhism separation]] (神仏分離) during the Meiji period, the deity was renamed to align with Shinto practices while retaining Benzaiten's legacy. The shrine is often considered one of the "[[Three Great Shrines of Benzaiten]]" (along with [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[Chikubu Island|Chikubushima Shrine]]) and is sometimes included among the "Five Great Benzaiten Shrines of Japan." == History == The shrine traces its origins to the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] and is closely associated with [[Shugendō]] practices and the legendary ascetic [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]], who established [[Ōmine-san]] as a sacred site. [[Kūkai]] (the founder of [[Kōyasan]]) also revered the shrine and reportedly spent three years here in spiritual practice before establishing [[Mount Kōya]]. The shrine's present structure dates back to the reign of [[:en:Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu]], who prayed for victory during the [[Jinshin War]]. Following his triumph, Emperor Tenmu contributed to the shrine’s expansion, solidifying its significance. Historically, the site was referred to as '''Biwazan Byakuhakuji''', a Buddhist temple dedicated to Benzaiten. During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], it was highly regarded as a place of worship for the [[Southern Court of Japan]], further solidifying its status as a spiritual hub. == Cultural Significance == The shrine is known for its unique connection to traditional [[Noh]] theater and music. It houses numerous offerings related to the performing arts, and its annual [[July 16]]–[[July 17]] festival includes sacred performances and rituals. The sacred statue of Benzaiten enshrined in the main hall is normally kept hidden and is only revealed during the annual grand festival. Another sacred statue, known as the "Sun Wheel Benzaiten," is unveiled every 60 years but was last displayed in 2008 as part of a special commemoration. == Sacred Site and Facilities == The shrine's grounds include: - The **Five Shrines Hall**, symbolizing cosmic harmony. - A path leading to the summit of [[Mount Misen]] (1,895 meters), which serves as the shrine's [[Okumiya|Okunomiya]] (inner sanctuary). == Important Cultural Properties == - **Wooden Noh and Kyōgen Masks**: A collection of 30 masks designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government. == Festivals and Rituals == Key events include: - **New Year Ceremony (January 1)**: Blessings for the year ahead. - **Misen Inner Shrine Festival (May)**: Rituals performed at the summit of Mount Misen. - **Grand Festival (July 16–17)**: Sacred performances and prayers. - **Autumn Festival (November 2)**: Celebrates harvest and gratitude. == Access == - By bus: From [[Shimoichiguchi Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], take the Nara Kotsu Bus to "Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine" (a11rox. 1 hour). - By car: Drive south along [[National Route 309]] and follow signs to the shrine. == See Also == - [[Biwazan Byakuhakuji]] (formerly affiliated with [[Kōfuku-ji]]) | Festival = [[:en:July 17|July 17]] }} '''Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine''' (天河大弁財天社), commonly referred to as '''Tenkawa Shrine''', is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the [[Yoshino District]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. The shrine is renowned for its deep association with the arts, music, and spirituality. It is a popular pilgrimage site for those in creative professions and seekers of spiritual insight. == Enshrined deity == The shrine's primary deity is [[Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto]], one of the [[Three Munakata Goddesses|Munakata Three Goddesses]]. Originally, the shrine was dedicated to [[Benzaiten]] (the Japanese Buddhist equivalent of the Hindu goddess [[Saraswati]]). Following the [[Shinto-Buddhism separation]] (神仏分離) during the Meiji period, the deity was renamed to align with Shinto practices while retaining Benzaiten's legacy. The shrine is often considered one of the "[[Three Great Shrines of Benzaiten]]" (along with [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[:en:Chikubu Island|Chikubushima Shrine]]) and is sometimes included among the "Five Great Benzaiten Shrines of Japan." == History == The shrine traces its origins to the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] and is closely associated with [[Shugendō]] practices and the legendary ascetic [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]], who established [[Ōmine-san]] as a sacred site. [[Kūkai]] (the founder of [[Kōyasan]]) also revered the shrine and reportedly spent three years here in spiritual practice before establishing [[Mount Kōya]]. The shrine's present structure dates back to the reign of [[:en:Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu]], who prayed for victory during the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]]. Following his triumph, Emperor Tenmu contributed to the shrine’s expansion, solidifying its significance. Historically, the site was referred to as '''Biwazan Byakuhakuji''', a Buddhist temple dedicated to Benzaiten. During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], it was highly regarded as a place of worship for the [[Southern Court of Japan]], further solidifying its status as a spiritual hub. == Cultural Significance == The shrine is known for its unique connection to traditional [[Noh]] theater and music. It houses numerous offerings related to the performing arts, and its annual [[:en:July 16|July 16]]–[[:en:July 17|July 17]] festival includes sacred performances and rituals. The sacred statue of Benzaiten enshrined in the main hall is normally kept hidden and is only revealed during the annual grand festival. Another sacred statue, known as the "Sun Wheel Benzaiten," is unveiled every 60 years but was last displayed in 2008 as part of a special commemoration. == Sacred Site and Facilities == The shrine's grounds include: - The **Five Shrines Hall**, symbolizing cosmic harmony. - A path leading to the summit of [[Mount Misen]] (1,895 meters), which serves as the shrine's [[Okumiya|Okunomiya]] (inner sanctuary). == Important Cultural Properties == - **Wooden Noh and Kyōgen Masks**: A collection of 30 masks designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government. == Festivals and Rituals == Key events include: - **New Year Ceremony (January 1)**: Blessings for the year ahead. - **Misen Inner Shrine Festival (May)**: Rituals performed at the summit of Mount Misen. - **Grand Festival (July 16–17)**: Sacred performances and prayers. - **Autumn Festival (November 2)**: Celebrates harvest and gratitude. == Access == - By bus: From [[Shimoichiguchi Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Yoshino Line|Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], take the Nara Kotsu Bus to "Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine" (a11rox. 1 hour). - By car: Drive south along [[National Route 309]] and follow signs to the shrine. == See Also == - [[Ōmine Mountains]] - [[Shugendō]] - [[Benzaiten]] - [[Yoshino-Kumano National Park]] == External Links == * [http://www.tenkawa-jinja.or.jp/ Official website] * [http://www.naraken-jinjacho.jp/jinja13.html Nara Prefecture Shrine Directory] {{Munakata Faith}} {{translated page|ja|天河大弁財天社|version=100692558|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shugendō]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[sn:Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine]] [[ja:天河大弁財天社]] [[Category:Buildings in Tenkawa, Nara]] [[Category:天川村の歴史]] [[Category:Emperor Tenmu]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Nara prefecture]] [[Category:En no Gyōja]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] {{Commons category|Tenkawa-dai-Benzaiten-sha}}

Torii gate {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|frame-height=250|coord={{coord|34|13|24|N|135|50|30|E}}}} | location = [[Tsubouchi]], [[Tenkawa]], [[Yoshino District]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]], Japan | Latitude degrees = 34| Latitude minutes = 13| Latitude seconds = 24.0 | Longitude degrees = 135| Longitude minutes = 50| Longitude seconds = 30.0 | deity = [[Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto]] | Shrine Rank = Yoshino General Shrine
Ōmine Head Shrine ([[Shugendō]])
[[Gōsha]] (local shrine) in Shinto | consecration_year = Unknown | Main hall = | Alternate name = Ōmine Head Shrine, Yoshino-Kumano Shrine (intermediate between Yoshino and Kumano)
Tenkawa Benzaiten, Tsubouchi Benzaiten Shrine
[[Biwazan Byakuhakuji]] (formerly affiliated with [[Kōfuku-ji]]) | Festival = [[:en:July 17|July 17]] }} '''Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine''' (天河大弁財天社), commonly referred to as '''Tenkawa Shrine''', is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the [[Yoshino District]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. The shrine is renowned for its deep association with the arts, music, and spirituality. It is a popular pilgrimage site for those in creative professions and seekers of spiritual insight. == Enshrined deity == The shrine's primary deity is [[Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto]], one of the [[Three Munakata Goddesses|Munakata Three Goddesses]]. Originally, the shrine was dedicated to [[Benzaiten]] (the Japanese Buddhist equivalent of the Hindu goddess [[Saraswati]]). Following the [[Shinto-Buddhism separation]] (神仏分離) during the Meiji period, the deity was renamed to align with Shinto practices while retaining Benzaiten's legacy. The shrine is often considered one of the "[[Three Great Shrines of Benzaiten]]" (along with [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[:en:Chikubu Island|Chikubushima Shrine]]) and is sometimes included among the "Five Great Benzaiten Shrines of Japan." == History == The shrine traces its origins to the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] and is closely associated with [[Shugendō]] practices and the legendary ascetic [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]], who established [[Ōmine-san]] as a sacred site. [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] (the founder of [[:en:Kōyasan|Kōyasan]]) also revered the shrine and reportedly spent three years here in spiritual practice before establishing [[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]]. The shrine's present structure dates back to the reign of [[:en:Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu]], who prayed for victory during the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]]. Following his triumph, Emperor Tenmu contributed to the shrine’s expansion, solidifying its significance. Historically, the site was referred to as '''Biwazan Byakuhakuji''', a Buddhist temple dedicated to Benzaiten. During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], it was highly regarded as a place of worship for the [[Southern Court of Japan]], further solidifying its status as a spiritual hub. == Cultural Significance == The shrine is known for its unique connection to traditional [[Noh]] theater and music. It houses numerous offerings related to the performing arts, and its annual [[:en:July 16|July 16]]–[[:en:July 17|July 17]] festival includes sacred performances and rituals. The sacred statue of Benzaiten enshrined in the main hall is normally kept hidden and is only revealed during the annual grand festival. Another sacred statue, known as the "Sun Wheel Benzaiten," is unveiled every 60 years but was last displayed in 2008 as part of a special commemoration. == Sacred Site and Facilities == The shrine's grounds include: - The **Five Shrines Hall**, symbolizing cosmic harmony. - A path leading to the summit of [[:en:Mount Misen|Mount Misen]] (1,895 meters), which serves as the shrine's [[Okumiya|Okunomiya]] (inner sanctuary). == Important Cultural Properties == - **Wooden Noh and Kyōgen Masks**: A collection of 30 masks designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government. == Festivals and Rituals == Key events include: - **New Year Ceremony (January 1)**: Blessings for the year ahead. - **Misen Inner Shrine Festival (May)**: Rituals performed at the summit of Mount Misen. - **Grand Festival (July 16–17)**: Sacred performances and prayers. - **Autumn Festival (November 2)**: Celebrates harvest and gratitude. == Access == - By bus: From [[Shimoichiguchi Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Yoshino Line|Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], take the Nara Kotsu Bus to "Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine" (a11rox. 1 hour). - By car: Drive south along [[:en:National Route 309|National Route 309]] and follow signs to the shrine. == See Also == - [[Ōmine Mountains]] - [[Shugendō]] - [[Benzaiten]] - [[Yoshino-Kumano National Park]] == External Links == * [http://www.tenkawa-jinja.or.jp/ Official website] * [http://www.naraken-jinjacho.jp/jinja13.html Nara Prefecture Shrine Directory] {{Munakata Faith}} {{translated page|ja|天河大弁財天社|version=100692558|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shugendō]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[sn:Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine]] [[ja:天河大弁財天社]] [[Category:Buildings in Tenkawa, Nara]] [[Category:天川村の歴史]] [[Category:Emperor Tenmu]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Nara prefecture]] [[Category:En no Gyōja]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] {{Commons category|Tenkawa-dai-Benzaiten-sha}}

**Misen Inner Shrine Festival (May)**: Rituals performed at the summit of Mount Misen. - **Grand Festival (July 16–17)**: Sacred performances and prayers. - **Autumn Festival (November 2)**: Celebrates harvest and gratitude. == Access == - By bus: From [[Shimoichiguchi Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Yoshino Line|Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], take the Nara Kotsu Bus to "Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine" (a11rox. 1 hour). - By car: Drive south along [[:en:National Route 309|National Route 309]] and follow signs to the shrine. == See Also == - Tenkawa Benzaiten, Tsubouchi Benzaiten Shrine
[[Biwazan Byakuhakuji]] (formerly affiliated with [[Kōfuku-ji]]) | Festival = [[:en:July 17|July 17]] }} '''Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine''' (天河大弁財天社), commonly referred to as '''Tenkawa Shrine''', is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the [[:en:Yoshino District|Yoshino District]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. The shrine is renowned for its deep association with the arts, music, and spirituality. It is a popular pilgrimage site for those in creative professions and seekers of spiritual insight. == Enshrined deity == The shrine's primary deity is [[Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto]], one of the [[Three Munakata Goddesses|Munakata Three Goddesses]]. Originally, the shrine was dedicated to [[Benzaiten]] (the Japanese Buddhist equivalent of the Hindu goddess [[Saraswati]]). Following the [[Shinto-Buddhism separation]] (神仏分離) during the Meiji period, the deity was renamed to align with Shinto practices while retaining Benzaiten's legacy. The shrine is often considered one of the "[[Three Great Shrines of Benzaiten]]" (along with [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[:en:Chikubu Island|Chikubushima Shrine]]) and is sometimes included among the "Five Great Benzaiten Shrines of Japan." == History == The shrine traces its origins to the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] and is closely associated with [[Shugendō]] practices and the legendary ascetic [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]], who established [[Ōmine-san]] as a sacred site. [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] (the founder of [[:en:Kōyasan|Kōyasan]]) also revered the shrine and reportedly spent three years here in spiritual practice before establishing [[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]]. The shrine's present structure dates back to the reign of [[:en:Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu]], who prayed for victory during the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]]. Following his triumph, Emperor Tenmu contributed to the shrine’s expansion, solidifying its significance. Historically, the site was referred to as '''Biwazan Byakuhakuji''', a Buddhist temple dedicated to Benzaiten. During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], it was highly regarded as a place of worship for the [[Southern Court of Japan]], further solidifying its status as a spiritual hub. == Cultural Significance == The shrine is known for its unique connection to traditional [[:en:Noh|Noh]] theater and music. It houses numerous offerings related to the performing arts, and its annual [[:en:July 16|July 16]]–[[:en:July 17|July 17]] festival includes sacred performances and rituals. The sacred statue of Benzaiten enshrined in the main hall is normally kept hidden and is only revealed during the annual grand festival. Another sacred statue, known as the "Sun Wheel Benzaiten," is unveiled every 60 years but was last displayed in 2008 as part of a special commemoration. == Sacred Site and Facilities == The shrine's grounds include: - The **Five Shrines Hall**, symbolizing cosmic harmony. - A path leading to the summit of [[:en:Mount Misen|Mount Misen]] (1,895 meters), which serves as the shrine's [[Okumiya|Okunomiya]] (inner sanctuary). == Important Cultural Properties == - **Wooden Noh and Kyōgen Masks**: A collection of 30 masks designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government. == Festivals and Rituals == Key events include: - **New Year Ceremony (January 1)**: Blessings for the year ahead. - **Misen Inner Shrine Festival (May)**: Rituals performed at the summit of Mount Misen. - **Grand Festival (July 16–17)**: Sacred performances and prayers. - **Autumn Festival (November 2)**: Celebrates harvest and gratitude. == Access == - By bus: From [[:en:Shimoichiguchi Station|Shimoichiguchi Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Yoshino Line|Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], take the Nara Kotsu Bus to "Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine" (a11rox. 1 hour). - By car: Drive south along [[:en:National Route 309|National Route 309]] and follow signs to the shrine. == See Also == - [[Ōmine Mountains]] - [[Shugendō]] - [[Benzaiten]] - [[:en:Yoshino-Kumano National Park|Yoshino-Kumano National Park]] == External Links == * [http://www.tenkawa-jinja.or.jp/ Official website] * [http://www.naraken-jinjacho.jp/jinja13.html Nara Prefecture Shrine Directory] {{Munakata Faith}} {{translated page|ja|天河大弁財天社|version=100692558|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shugendō]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[sn:Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine]] [[ja:天河大弁財天社]] [[Category:Buildings in Tenkawa, Nara]] [[Category:天川村の歴史]] [[Category:Emperor Tenmu]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Nara prefecture]] [[Category:En no Gyōja]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] {{Commons category|Tenkawa-dai-Benzaiten-sha}}

[[Biwazan Byakuhakuji]] (formerly affiliated with [[Kōfuku-ji]]) | Festival = [[:en:July 17|July 17]] }} '''Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine''' (天河大弁財天社), commonly referred to as '''Tenkawa Shrine''', is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in the [[:en:Yoshino District|Yoshino District]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. The shrine is renowned for its deep association with the arts, music, and spirituality. It is a popular pilgrimage site for those in creative professions and seekers of spiritual insight. == Enshrined deity == The shrine's primary deity is [[Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto]], one of the [[Three Munakata Goddesses|Munakata Three Goddesses]]. Originally, the shrine was dedicated to [[Benzaiten]] (the Japanese Buddhist equivalent of the Hindu goddess [[Saraswati]]). Following the [[Shinto-Buddhism separation]] (神仏分離) during the Meiji period, the deity was renamed to align with Shinto practices while retaining Benzaiten's legacy. The shrine is often considered one of the "[[:en:Three Great Shrines of Benzaiten|Three Great Shrines of Benzaiten]]" (along with [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[:en:Chikubu Island|Chikubushima Shrine]]) and is sometimes included among the "Five Great Benzaiten Shrines of Japan." == History == The shrine traces its origins to the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] and is closely associated with [[Shugendō]] practices and the legendary ascetic [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]], who established [[Ōmine-san]] as a sacred site. [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] (the founder of [[:en:Kōyasan|Kōyasan]]) also revered the shrine and reportedly spent three years here in spiritual practice before establishing [[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]]. The shrine's present structure dates back to the reign of [[:en:Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu]], who prayed for victory during the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]]. Following his triumph, Emperor Tenmu contributed to the shrine’s expansion, solidifying its significance. Historically, the site was referred to as '''Biwazan Byakuhakuji''', a Buddhist temple dedicated to Benzaiten. During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], it was highly regarded as a place of worship for the [[Southern Court of Japan]], further solidifying its status as a spiritual hub. == Cultural Significance == The shrine is known for its unique connection to traditional [[:en:Noh|Noh]] theater and music. It houses numerous offerings related to the performing arts, and its annual [[:en:July 16|July 16]]–[[:en:July 17|July 17]] festival includes sacred performances and rituals. The sacred statue of Benzaiten enshrined in the main hall is normally kept hidden and is only revealed during the annual grand festival. Another sacred statue, known as the "Sun Wheel Benzaiten," is unveiled every 60 years but was last displayed in 2008 as part of a special commemoration. == Sacred Site and Facilities == The shrine's grounds include: - The **Five Shrines Hall**, symbolizing cosmic harmony. - A path leading to the summit of [[:en:Mount Misen|Mount Misen]] (1,895 meters), which serves as the shrine's [[Okumiya|Okunomiya]] (inner sanctuary). == Important Cultural Properties == - **Wooden Noh and Kyōgen Masks**: A collection of 30 masks designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government. == Festivals and Rituals == Key events include: - **New Year Ceremony (January 1)**: Blessings for the year ahead. - **Misen Inner Shrine Festival (May)**: Rituals performed at the summit of Mount Misen. - **Grand Festival (July 16–17)**: Sacred performances and prayers. - **Autumn Festival (November 2)**: Celebrates harvest and gratitude. == Access == - By bus: From [[:en:Shimoichiguchi Station|Shimoichiguchi Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Yoshino Line|Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], take the Nara Kotsu Bus to "Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine" (a11rox. 1 hour). - By car: Drive south along [[:en:National Route 309|National Route 309]] and follow signs to the shrine. == See Also == - [[Ōmine Mountains]] - [[Shugendō]] - [[Benzaiten]] - [[:en:Yoshino-Kumano National Park|Yoshino-Kumano National Park]] == External Links == * [http://www.tenkawa-jinja.or.jp/ Official website] * [http://www.naraken-jinjacho.jp/jinja13.html Nara Prefecture Shrine Directory] {{Munakata Faith}} {{translated page|ja|天河大弁財天社|version=100692558|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shugendō]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[sn:Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine]] [[ja:天河大弁財天社]] [[Category:Buildings in Tenkawa, Nara]] [[Category:History of Tenkawa Village]] [[Category:Emperor Tenmu]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Nara prefecture]] [[Category:En no Gyōja]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] {{Commons category|Tenkawa-dai-Benzaiten-sha}}

Another sacred statue, known as the "Sun Wheel Benzaiten," is unveiled every 60 years but was last displayed in 2008 as part of a special commemoration. == Sacred Site and Facilities == The shrine's grounds include: - The **Five Shrines Hall**, symbolizing cosmic harmony. - A path leading to the summit of [[:en:Mount Misen|Mount Misen]] (1,895 meters), which serves as the shrine's [[Okumiya|Okunomiya]] (inner sanctuary). == Important Cultural Properties == - **Wooden Noh and Kyōgen Masks**: A collection of 30 masks designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government. == Festivals and Rituals == Key events include: - **New Year Ceremony (January 1)**: Blessings for the year ahead. - **Misen Inner Shrine Festival (May)**: Rituals performed at the summit of Mount Misen. - **Grand Festival (July 16–17)**: Sacred performances and prayers. - **Autumn Festival (November 2)**: Celebrates harvest and gratitude. == Access == - By bus: From [[:en:Shimoichiguchi Station|Shimoichiguchi Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Yoshino Line|Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], take the Nara Kotsu Bus to "Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine" (a11rox. 1 hour). - By car: Drive south along [[:en:National Route 309|National Route 309]] and follow signs to the shrine. == See Also == - [[Ōmine Mountains]] - [[Shugendō]] - [[Benzaiten]] - [[:en:Yoshino-Kumano National Park|Yoshino-Kumano National Park]] == External Links == * [http://www.tenkawa-jinja.or.jp/ Official website] * [http://www.naraken-jinjacho.jp/jinja13.html Nara Prefecture Shrine Directory] {{Munakata Faith}} {{translated page|ja|天河大弁財天社|version=100692558|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shugendō]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[sn:Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine]] [[ja:天河大弁財天社]] [[Category:Buildings in Tenkawa, Nara]] [[Category:History of Tenkawa Village]] [[Category:Emperor Tenmu]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Nara prefecture]] [[Category:En no Gyōja]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] {{Commons category|Tenkawa-dai-Benzaiten-sha}} {{wikidata link|Q11442752}}

Ōmine Head Shrine ([[Shugendō]])
[[Gōsha]] (local shrine) in Shinto | consecration_year = Unknown | Main hall = | Alternate name = Ōmine Head Shrine, Yoshino-Kumano Shrine (intermediate between Yoshino and Kumano)
Tenkawa Benzaiten, Tsubouchi Benzaiten Shrine
[[Biwazan Byakuhakuji]] (formerly affiliated with [[Kōfuku-ji]]) | Festival = [[:en:July 17|July 17]] | location = {{ill|Tenkawa|qid=Q1203501}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34.22331248544997|N|135.84170253926723|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine''' (天河大弁財天社), commonly referred to as '''Tenkawa Shrine''', is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in the [[:en:Yoshino District|Yoshino District]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. The shrine is renowned for its deep association with the arts, music, and spirituality. It is a popular pilgrimage site for those in creative professions and seekers of spiritual insight. == Enshrined deity == The shrine's primary deity is [[Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto]], one of the [[Three Munakata Goddesses|Munakata Three Goddesses]]. Originally, the shrine was dedicated to [[Benzaiten]] (the Japanese Buddhist equivalent of the Hindu goddess [[Saraswati]]). Following the [[Shinto-Buddhism separation]] (神仏分離) during the Meiji period, the deity was renamed to align with Shinto practices while retaining Benzaiten's legacy. The shrine is often considered one of the "[[:en:Three Great Shrines of Benzaiten|Three Great Shrines of Benzaiten]]" (along with [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[:en:Chikubu Island|Chikubushima Shrine]]) and is sometimes included among the "Five Great Benzaiten Shrines of Japan." == History == The shrine traces its origins to the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] and is closely associated with [[Shugendō]] practices and the legendary ascetic [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]], who established [[Ōmine-san]] as a sacred site. [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] (the founder of [[:en:Kōyasan|Kōyasan]]) also revered the shrine and reportedly spent three years here in spiritual practice before establishing [[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]]. The shrine's present structure dates back to the reign of [[:en:Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu]], who prayed for victory during the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]]. Following his triumph, Emperor Tenmu contributed to the shrine’s expansion, solidifying its significance. Historically, the site was referred to as '''Biwazan Byakuhakuji''', a Buddhist temple dedicated to Benzaiten. During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], it was highly regarded as a place of worship for the [[Southern Court of Japan]], further solidifying its status as a spiritual hub. == Cultural Significance == The shrine is known for its unique connection to traditional [[:en:Noh|Noh]] theater and music. It houses numerous offerings related to the performing arts, and its annual [[:en:July 16|July 16]]–[[:en:July 17|July 17]] festival includes sacred performances and rituals. The sacred statue of Benzaiten enshrined in the main hall is normally kept hidden and is only revealed during the annual grand festival. Another sacred statue, known as the "Sun Wheel Benzaiten," is unveiled every 60 years but was last displayed in 2008 as part of a special commemoration. == Sacred Site and Facilities == The shrine's grounds include: - The **Five Shrines Hall**, symbolizing cosmic harmony. - A path leading to the summit of [[:en:Mount Misen|Mount Misen]] (1,895 meters), which serves as the shrine's [[Okumiya|Okunomiya]] (inner sanctuary). == Important Cultural Properties == - **Wooden Noh and Kyōgen Masks**: A collection of 30 masks designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government. == Festivals and Rituals == Key events include: - **New Year Ceremony (January 1)**: Blessings for the year ahead. - **Misen Inner Shrine Festival (May)**: Rituals performed at the summit of Mount Misen. - **Grand Festival (July 16–17)**: Sacred performances and prayers. - **Autumn Festival (November 2)**: Celebrates harvest and gratitude. == Access == - By bus: From [[:en:Shimoichiguchi Station|Shimoichiguchi Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Yoshino Line|Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], take the Nara Kotsu Bus to "Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine" (a11rox. 1 hour). - By car: Drive south along [[:en:National Route 309|National Route 309]] and follow signs to the shrine. == See Also == - [[Ōmine Mountains]] - [[Shugendō]] - [[Benzaiten]] - [[:en:Yoshino-Kumano National Park|Yoshino-Kumano National Park]] == External Links == * [http://www.tenkawa-jinja.or.jp/ Official website] * [http://www.naraken-jinjacho.jp/jinja13.html Nara Prefecture Shrine Directory] {{Munakata Faith}} {{translated page|ja|天河大弁財天社|version=100692558|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shugendō]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[ja:天河大弁財天社]] [[Category:Buildings in Tenkawa, Nara]] [[Category:History of Tenkawa Village]] [[Category:Emperor Tenmu]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Nara prefecture]] [[Category:En no Gyōja]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] {{Commons category|Tenkawa-dai-Benzaiten-sha}} {{wikidata link|Q11442752}}

Ōmine Head Shrine ([[Shugendō]])
[[Gōsha]] (local shrine) in Shinto | consecration_year = Unknown | Main hall = | Alternate name = Ōmine Head Shrine, Yoshino-Kumano Shrine (intermediate between Yoshino and Kumano)
Tenkawa Benzaiten, Tsubouchi Benzaiten Shrine
[[Biwazan Byakuhakuji]] (formerly affiliated with [[Kōfuku-ji]]) | Festival = [[:en:July 17|July 17]] | location = {{ill|Tenkawa|WD=Q1203501}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34.22331248544997|N|135.84170253926723|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine''' (天河大弁財天社), commonly referred to as '''Tenkawa Shrine''', is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in the [[:en:Yoshino District|Yoshino District]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. The shrine is renowned for its deep association with the arts, music, and spirituality. It is a popular pilgrimage site for those in creative professions and seekers of spiritual insight. == Enshrined deity == The shrine's primary deity is [[Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto]], one of the [[Three Munakata Goddesses|Munakata Three Goddesses]]. Originally, the shrine was dedicated to [[Benzaiten]] (the Japanese Buddhist equivalent of the Hindu goddess [[Saraswati]]). Following the [[Shinto-Buddhism separation]] (神仏分離) during the Meiji period, the deity was renamed to align with Shinto practices while retaining Benzaiten's legacy. The shrine is often considered one of the "[[:en:Three Great Shrines of Benzaiten|Three Great Shrines of Benzaiten]]" (along with [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[:en:Chikubu Island|Chikubushima Shrine]]) and is sometimes included among the "Five Great Benzaiten Shrines of Japan." == History == The shrine traces its origins to the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] and is closely associated with [[Shugendō]] practices and the legendary ascetic [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]], who established [[Ōmine-san]] as a sacred site. [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] (the founder of [[:en:Kōyasan|Kōyasan]]) also revered the shrine and reportedly spent three years here in spiritual practice before establishing [[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]]. The shrine's present structure dates back to the reign of [[:en:Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu]], who prayed for victory during the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]]. Following his triumph, Emperor Tenmu contributed to the shrine’s expansion, solidifying its significance. Historically, the site was referred to as '''Biwazan Byakuhakuji''', a Buddhist temple dedicated to Benzaiten. During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], it was highly regarded as a place of worship for the [[Southern Court of Japan]], further solidifying its status as a spiritual hub. == Cultural Significance == The shrine is known for its unique connection to traditional [[:en:Noh|Noh]] theater and music. It houses numerous offerings related to the performing arts, and its annual [[:en:July 16|July 16]]–[[:en:July 17|July 17]] festival includes sacred performances and rituals. The sacred statue of Benzaiten enshrined in the main hall is normally kept hidden and is only revealed during the annual grand festival. Another sacred statue, known as the "Sun Wheel Benzaiten," is unveiled every 60 years but was last displayed in 2008 as part of a special commemoration. == Sacred Site and Facilities == The shrine's grounds include: - The **Five Shrines Hall**, symbolizing cosmic harmony. - A path leading to the summit of [[:en:Mount Misen|Mount Misen]] (1,895 meters), which serves as the shrine's [[Okumiya|Okunomiya]] (inner sanctuary). == Important Cultural Properties == - **Wooden Noh and Kyōgen Masks**: A collection of 30 masks designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government. == Festivals and Rituals == Key events include: - **New Year Ceremony (January 1)**: Blessings for the year ahead. - **Misen Inner Shrine Festival (May)**: Rituals performed at the summit of Mount Misen. - **Grand Festival (July 16–17)**: Sacred performances and prayers. - **Autumn Festival (November 2)**: Celebrates harvest and gratitude. == Access == - By bus: From [[:en:Shimoichiguchi Station|Shimoichiguchi Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Yoshino Line|Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], take the Nara Kotsu Bus to "Tenkawa Daibenzaiten Shrine" (a11rox. 1 hour). - By car: Drive south along [[:en:National Route 309|National Route 309]] and follow signs to the shrine. == See Also == - [[Ōmine Mountains]] - [[Shugendō]] - [[Benzaiten]] - [[:en:Yoshino-Kumano National Park|Yoshino-Kumano National Park]] == External Links == * [http://www.tenkawa-jinja.or.jp/ Official website] * [http://www.naraken-jinjacho.jp/jinja13.html Nara Prefecture Shrine Directory] {{Munakata Faith}} {{translated page|ja|天河大弁財天社|version=100692558|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shugendō]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[ja:天河大弁財天社]] [[Category:Buildings in Tenkawa, Nara]] [[Category:History of Tenkawa Village]] [[Category:Emperor Tenmu]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Nara prefecture]] [[Category:En no Gyōja]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] {{Commons category|Tenkawa-dai-Benzaiten-sha}} {{wikidata link|Q11442752}}
